Q1: I tried to keep things structured, usually blocking out time on weekends and a couple of evenings during the week. Being 29 and having other responsibilities, I needed to be intentional about when I studied. I'm a hands-on learner, so I'd often set up practical exercises or work through problems physically rather than just reading. I aimed for around 13 hours a week, though some weeks were tighter than others depending on what else was going on in my life.
Q2: My pattern was somewhat regular but definitely flexed around deadlines and my work schedule. I tried to maintain consistency because I know that's what works for me, but life at 29 doesn't always cooperate. When big assignments were due, I'd ramp up significantly. Otherwise, I kept a steady rhythm of checking in and doing the work throughout the week.
Q3: Usually it was either checking for new announcements, looking at upcoming deadlines, or connecting with my peer study group. I logged in pretty frequently—probably almost daily during active weeks. Sometimes I'd just pop in to see if anyone had posted in the discussions or if there was feedback on something I'd submitted. The peer support aspect really kept me coming back.
Q4: Yeah, after the first few weeks it became automatic. I'd grab my coffee in the morning and check the LMS like checking email. The peer interactions really helped cement this habit—knowing that classmates might have responded to something or posted a question I could help with made it feel more like a community than just a platform. It became part of my daily rhythm.
Q5: The discussion forums and peer interaction features were genuinely valuable to me. I connected with 24 different peers throughout the course, and those conversations helped me understand concepts better than just watching videos alone. The video content was useful too—I watched over 160 minutes worth—especially when I could pause and try things hands-on. Having that combination of visual instruction and peer dialogue really suited my learning style.
Q6: I'll be honest, I didn't really engage with educational technology tools outside the basic LMS. Some of the supplementary reading materials I skimmed rather than deeply engaged with. If something felt too theoretical without practical application, I'd deprioritize it. I learn by doing, so resources that were just walls of text didn't stick with me as well.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time when I could see the direct connection to assignments or real-world application. If I could physically work through a problem or discuss it with peers and see different perspectives, that felt valuable. The peer support intervention really highlighted this for me—collaborating made even difficult content feel worthwhile because we were figuring it out together.
Q8: Optional activities felt like opportunities rather than obligations, which actually made me more curious about them sometimes. Required work had a different weight—more pressure, more anxiety. But I found myself engaging more deeply with optional peer discussions because there was no grade attached, just genuine learning. The required stuff I completed thoroughly—my assignment completion was solid—but optional stuff felt freer.
Q9: Absolutely. I'm very kinesthetic, so I'd download materials and work through problems with pen and paper, or I'd set up physical scenarios to understand concepts. I used external YouTube videos, printed out diagrams, and worked at a whiteboard in my apartment. A lot of my actual learning happened away from the screen, manipulating things with my hands.
Q10: There were many times I'd be at my desk for hours working through problems manually after just briefly accessing a PDF on the LMS. Or I'd have a study session with peers over video call that wasn't captured anywhere in the system. My deep processing happened offline—the LMS saw me download something once, but not the three hours I spent afterwards really wrestling with it.
Q11: Constantly. I'd download lecture slides or problem sets once, print them out, and work on them for days. I'm not someone who likes studying on a screen if I can avoid it. So my single download would turn into a week of offline engagement. The LMS would show minimal activity during my most intensive study periods.
Q12: Around exam time, things got really difficult for me. I was surprised by how poorly I performed on the exam despite feeling like I understood the material. My attendance was good, my assignments were strong, but something wasn't translating to exam performance. That was a hard moment—I felt like I'd put in the work but the results didn't reflect it.
Q13: Fatigue definitely played a role, especially being older and balancing more responsibilities than typical students. Some weeks I'd access materials but be too mentally drained to really absorb them. My stress levels stayed pretty low overall, which helped, but there were definitely times when competing responsibilities meant I was going through the motions rather than deeply engaging.
Q14: Yes, particularly with video content sometimes. I'd have it playing but my mind would wander, or I'd be doing something else simultaneously. Those 163 minutes of video watching weren't all quality engagement—some of it was background noise while I was dealing with other things. I tried to be honest with myself about when I was really learning versus just being present.
Q15: I postponed exam preparation, honestly. I was confident in my assignment work and felt like I understood things, so I didn't prioritize exam-specific studying until it was too late. Looking back, I should have practiced more under exam conditions. I avoided timed practice tests because they made me anxious, and that avoidance cost me.
Q16: Deadlines were my anchor points. I'd work backwards from due dates and structure my week around them. My assignment completion rate was solid because I respected deadlines, but I realize now I was too focused on assignment deadlines and not enough on building exam-ready knowledge. The LMS deadline reminders kept me on track for submissions but didn't help with the bigger picture.
Q17: Feedback on assignments was encouraging—I scored well and that motivated me to keep engaging. But I wonder now if that positive feedback gave me false confidence about my overall understanding. When I did well on assignments, I'd feel good and maybe not dig deeper into areas where I was actually weak. The feedback loop was positive but maybe not diagnostic enough.
Q18: The course structure with regular assignments kept me consistently engaged throughout the semester. I appreciated having frequent touchpoints rather than just a midterm and final. The peer support intervention was particularly impactful—being connected with other students gave the course a social dimension that kept me motivated even when content was challenging.
Q19: The peer interaction features made engagement much easier for me. Having 24 different peer connections meant there was always someone to discuss things with. What made it harder was the exam format—I did well with practical assignments but struggled with the timed exam environment. The course design favored my strengths in some ways but exposed my weaknesses in others.
Q20: I think that could describe someone like me, actually. I was highly active in the LMS—logging in frequently, watching videos, participating in discussions—but my exam submission didn't reflect that engagement. Sometimes high activity means you're struggling and seeking help, or you're engaged socially but not mastering the content in ways that translate to formal assessments.
Q21: That makes sense to me because so much real learning happens offline. Someone could download materials once, study intensively from printed notes, and ace the exam without the LMS knowing. I was the opposite—high LMS activity but my exam didn't go well. Activity and performance aren't the same thing, and the LMS only sees one dimension.
Q22: Course logs can accurately show patterns of access, timing, and social engagement like discussion posts. They capture when you're present in the system. But they completely miss the quality of that engagement, offline study, the depth of understanding, and whether someone is actually learning versus just clicking through. They see behavior, not cognition.
Q23: Being engaged means genuinely wrestling with the material, asking questions, connecting with peers, and trying to apply what you're learning. It's not just showing up—it's being mentally present and curious. For me, engagement meant those peer discussions where we'd debate concepts, or the hours I spent working through problems by hand. It's active participation in your own learning.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how much I learned from my peer study group in ways that didn't happen on the platform. It wouldn't capture my physical, hands-on study methods or the genuine connections I made with classmates. And it definitely wouldn't show my surprise and disappointment at failing the exam despite feeling engaged all semester. The emotional journey of trying hard and still falling short—that's invisible to the data.
